title: Hiyoshi Taisha

infobox.value: (日吉大社) is a [Shinto shrine](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine") located in the city of [Ōtsu](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsu "Ōtsu"), [Shiga Prefecture](/wiki/Shiga_Prefecture "Shiga Prefecture") [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"). This shrine is one of the [Twenty-Two Shrines](/wiki/Twenty-Two_Shrines "Twenty-Two Shrines"). Known before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") as **Hiei Taisha** (日枝大社) or **Hie jinja**, "Hiyoshi" is now the preferred spelling. It was also known as the [Sanno Gongen](/wiki/Oyamakui_no_Kami#Sanno_Gongen "Oyamakui no Kami") (山王権現). The head shrine in Ōtsu heads the seventh largest [shrine network](/wiki/Shinto_shrine#Shrine_networks "Shinto shrine") in Japan, with approximately 3800 Hiyoshi, Hie, and Sannō shrines nationwide. [Torii](/wiki/Torii "Torii") of this shrine have a distinctive configuration, known as the "[Sannō torii](/wiki/Sann%C5%8D_torii "Sannō torii")", with a gaggle above the main crossbeam. The 400,000 square meter precincts centered is designated as a [National Historic Site](/wiki/Monuments_of_Japan "Monuments of Japan"),[[1]](#cite_note-Bunka1-1) and the east and west main shrine buildings, the Nishi Hongū (西本宮) and Higashi Hongū (東本宮) are designated as [National Treasures](/wiki/National_Treasures_of_Japan "National Treasures of Japan"),[[2]](#cite_note-Bunka2-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Bunka3-3) and many of the structures in the precincts are designated as National [Important Cultural Properties](/wiki/Important_Cultural_Property_(Japan) "Important Cultural Property (Japan)").

Higashi Hongū (East Hall of Worship)

**Hiyoshi Taisha** (日吉大社) is a [Shinto shrine](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine") located in the city of [Ōtsu](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsu "Ōtsu"), [Shiga Prefecture](/wiki/Shiga_Prefecture "Shiga Prefecture") [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"). This shrine is one of the [Twenty-Two Shrines](/wiki/Twenty-Two_Shrines "Twenty-Two Shrines"). Known before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") as **Hiei Taisha** (日枝大社) or **Hie jinja**, "Hiyoshi" is now the preferred spelling. It was also known as the [Sanno Gongen](/wiki/Oyamakui_no_Kami#Sanno_Gongen "Oyamakui no Kami") (山王権現). The head shrine in Ōtsu heads the seventh largest [shrine network](/wiki/Shinto_shrine#Shrine_networks "Shinto shrine") in Japan, with approximately 3800 Hiyoshi, Hie, and Sannō shrines nationwide. [Torii](/wiki/Torii "Torii") of this shrine have a distinctive configuration, known as the "[Sannō torii](/wiki/Sann%C5%8D_torii "Sannō torii")", with a gaggle above the main crossbeam. The 400,000 square meter precincts centered is designated as a [National Historic Site](/wiki/Monuments_of_Japan "Monuments of Japan"),[[1]](#cite_note-Bunka1-1) and the east and west main shrine buildings, the Nishi Hongū (西本宮) and Higashi Hongū (東本宮) are designated as [National Treasures](/wiki/National_Treasures_of_Japan "National Treasures of Japan"),[[2]](#cite_note-Bunka2-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Bunka3-3) and many of the structures in the precincts are designated as National [Important Cultural Properties](/wiki/Important_Cultural_Property_(Japan) "Important Cultural Property (Japan)").

## Enshrined *kami*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hiyoshi_Taisha&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Enshrined kami")]

Main shrine

* Nishi Hongū: [Ōnamuchi](/wiki/%C5%8Ckuninushi "Ōkuninushi") (大己貴神)
* Higashi Hongū: [Ōyamakui](/wiki/Oyamakui_no_Kami "Oyamakui no Kami") (大山咋神)

Subsidiary shrines

* Ushio-gū: Ōyamakui *aramitama* (大山咋神荒魂)
* Juge Jinja: Tamakushi-hime (鴨玉依姫命)
* Sannomiya-gū: Tamakushi-hime *aramitama* (鴨玉依姫命荒魂)
* Usa-gū: Takiri-hime (田心姫神)
* Shirayama-gū: Kikurihime (白山姫神, *Shirayama-hime*)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hiyoshi_Taisha&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The first mention of the *[kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")* Oyamakui is recorded in [Kojiki](/wiki/Kojiki "Kojiki"), written in the 8th century AD, which states that this god resides at [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei"), which is located immediately to the east of Hiyoshi Taisha. This *kami* was relocated from the summit of the mountain to its present location in the seventh year of the reign of the semi-legendary [Emperor Sujin](/wiki/Emperor_Sujin "Emperor Sujin"), or 90 BC per the traditional calendar. In 668 AD, [Emperor Tenji](/wiki/Emperor_Tenji "Emperor Tenji") decided to relocate the capital to [Ōmi Province](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Province "Ōmi Province") and built the [Ōtsu Palace](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsu_Palace "Ōtsu Palace"). At this time, the *kami* of [Ōmiwa Shrine](/wiki/%C5%8Cmiwa_Shrine "Ōmiwa Shrine") in [Yamato Province](/wiki/Yamato_Province "Yamato Province") (who served as protector of the imperial dynasty) was relocated as well, and was installed in the Nishi Hongū, whereas the original sanctuary came to be called the Higashi Hongū. In 788 AD, [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō") erected the [Tendai](/wiki/Tendai "Tendai") [Buddhist temple](/wiki/Japanese_Buddhism "Japanese Buddhism") complex of [Enryaku-ji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji") on Mount Hiei. After the transfer of the capital to [Heian-kyō](/wiki/Heian-ky%C5%8D "Heian-kyō"), Enryaku-ji and by extension, Hiyoshi Taisha came to be guardians of the spiritually vulnerable northeast quadrant from the capital. As Enryaku-ji became ever more powerful, and the Buddhist faith gradually amalgamated with Shinto under the *[Shinbutsu-shūgō](/wiki/Shinbutsu-sh%C5%ABg%C5%8D "Shinbutsu-shūgō")* policy, Hiyoshi Taisha was subsumed into Enryaku-ji. As missionaries from Enryaku-ji built Buddhist temples all across Japan, they also spread the faith in the "[Sanno Gongen](/wiki/Oyamakui_no_Kami#Sanno_Gongen "Oyamakui no Kami")" and the Hie *kami*.

The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period").[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 965, [Emperor Murakami](/wiki/Emperor_Murakami "Emperor Murakami") ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian *kami* of Japan,[[5]](#cite_note-pf118-5) and Hie Taisha was added to this listing by [Emperor Go-Suzaku](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Suzaku "Emperor Go-Suzaku") in 1039. This unique number of Imperial-designated shrines has not been altered since that time.[[5]](#cite_note-pf118-5)

During the late [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period"), political troubles arose between Enryaku-ji and the secular government in Kyoto. It became the practice of the [warrior-monks](/wiki/S%C5%8Dhei "Sōhei") from the temple to carry a *[mikoshi](/wiki/Mikoshi "Mikoshi")* portable shrine from Hie Taisha into the capital and to riot to enforce their political will. Soon after the *[kanpaku](/wiki/Kanpaku "Kanpaku")* [Fujiwara no Moromichi](/wiki/Fujiwara_no_Moromichi "Fujiwara no Moromichi") dared to oppose the riots by stopping the *mikoshi*, he died under mysterious circumstances, which the temple was quick to attribute to a curse from the gods for having shown disrespect to the *mikoshi*. The *mikoshi* of Hei Taisha thus became an object of fear and awe, and the temple used the tactic of running riot with the *mikoshi* to obtain their will on more than 40 occasions over a 370+ year period into the [Sengoku period](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period").

The practice was stopped in 1571 when [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") ordered Enryaku-ji to be razed to the ground, and all of its monks to be massacred. This also included Hie Taisha. The shrine was rebuilt under [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi"), with the oldest buildings currently at the shrine dating from the period of 1586 to 1597. Toyotomi Hideyoshi had a deep faith in the [Sanno Gongen](/wiki/Oyamakui_no_Kami#Sanno_Gongen "Oyamakui no Kami"), as his childhood name was "Hiyoshi Maru" and his nickname was "monkey", an animal which was considered to be the [spiritual messenger](/wiki/Shinshi "Shinshi") of the Hie *kami*. The Nishi Hongū was reconstructed in 1586 and the Higashi Hongū in 1595. [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") also had faith in the [Sanno Gongen](/wiki/Oyamakui_no_Kami#Sanno_Gongen "Oyamakui no Kami") and the shrine was supported by the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate").

In 1868 (the first year after the [Meiji restoration](/wiki/Meiji_restoration "Meiji restoration")), the new [Meiji government](/wiki/Meiji_government "Meiji government") decreed the [separation of Shinto and Buddhism](/wiki/Shinbutsu_bunri "Shinbutsu bunri"). Hie Taisha was at the forefront of this effort, and was one of the first to burn or otherwise dispose of its Buddhist statuary, ritual implements and scriptures. This was the beginning of the nationwide *[Haibutsu kishaku](/wiki/Haibutsu_kishaku "Haibutsu kishaku")* movement. Under [State Shinto](/wiki/State_Shinto "State Shinto"), the shrive was officially designated one of the *[Kanpei-taisha](/wiki/Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_Shrines "Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines")* (官幣大社), or Imperial shrine of the first rank.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
